question: Modify the `Book` class to include a destructor. The destructor should print a message indicating the title of the book object that is being destroyed.

test_cases.expected:
--- Starting Readability/Efficiency Test ---
Book 'The Hobbit' (ID: 101) constructed.
Book 'The Hobbit' (ID: 101) copied constructed from ID 101.
Book 'Effective C++' (ID: 102) constructed.
Book 'Effective C++' (ID: 102) copy assigned from ID 102.
Book 'Effective C++' (ID: 102) is being destroyed.
Book 'Clean Code' (ID: 103) constructed.
Book 'The Hobbit' (ID: 101) copy assigned from ID 101.
--- Populating vector ---
Book 'The Pragmatic Programmer' (ID: 104) constructed.
Book 'The Pragmatic Programmer' (ID: 104) copied constructed from ID 104.
Book 'The Pragmatic Programmer' (ID: 104) is being destroyed.
Book 'Design Patterns' (ID: 105) constructed.
Book 'I, Robot' (ID: 106) constructed.
Book 'I, Robot' (ID: 106) copied constructed from ID 106.
Book 'I, Robot' (ID: 106) is being destroyed.

Inside temporary block.
Book 'Temporary Read' (ID: 107) is being destroyed.
Outside temporary block.
--- Readability/Efficiency Test Ended ---
Book 'I, Robot' (ID: 106) is being destroyed.
Book 'Design Patterns' (ID: 105) is being destroyed.
Book 'The Pragmatic Programmer' (ID: 104) is being destroyed.
Book 'The Hobbit' (ID: 101) is being destroyed.
Book 'Effective C++' (ID: 102) is being destroyed.
Book 'The Hobbit' (ID: 101) is being destroyed.

errors: Using raw `char*` for string management instead of `std::string` makes the code less readable, more verbose, and significantly more prone to memory errors if the Rule of Three/Five is not meticulously followed.

Manual implementation of the copy constructor, copy assignment operator, and destructor for `char*` adds unnecessary boilerplate and complexity compared to relying on `std::string`'s automatic and robust memory management.
While functionally correct, this approach is less efficient for typical string operations than `std::string` and introduces overhead for manual resource handling.
